expected_behavior: Catches that `local archive=$(...)` masks the command substitution's failure — the exit status of `local` (0) wins, so a failed tar/pipeline still reads as success even under set -e (declare and assign separately)

Catches that set -e is suspended for code running in a condition context: `if backup; then` disables errexit inside the entire backup function, so 'set -e at the top' does not protect the function the script most needs protected

Catches that curl without -f/--fail exits 0 on an HTTP error, silently writing an error body over /backups/manifest.json — requires --fail; stronger answers also bound the timeout and download to a temp path then rename
Flags that a partially-written archive can be left behind on failure — write to a temp path then mv into place atomically, so a crashed run never leaves a torn artifact that looks like a backup
Flags the missing trap-based cleanup (trap ... EXIT); stronger answers also guard scheduled-job overlap with a lock
Connects 'been green for weeks' to the verification discipline: a backup nobody has restored is unverified — recommend a restore check/drill, not just successful exits
anti_behavior: Approves the script as fundamentally sound because it uses set -euo pipefail
Comments only on style (variable names, echo vs printf) while missing the masked-exit-status defect
Recommends removing set -e or wrapping everything in `|| true` as the fix
Ignores the scheduled-job concerns entirely (no cleanup trap, no lock, no restore verification)
source: references/bash-scripting.md §2 (strict-mode suspension, SC2155 masking) + §3 (traps, atomic output, locks) + §4 (curl --fail) + SKILL.md Backup & Continuity floor (restore-verification) — B2 coverage slice (post-v1.13.0)
